question: Compare the RCS volumes from Table 5.1-1 with the flow path shown in Figure 5.1-2, and explain how the volume distribution supports natural circulation.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core by natural circulation and interfaces with several other systems for safety and operational functions, including degasification and emergency core cooling. Its pressure-retaining parts help maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, serving as a barrier to radionuclide release.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry, and ensures uniform boron concentration to protect fuel integrity during all operating conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the Reactor Coolant System’s high-point vents, specifically focusing on the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components and actuation valves. It details compliance with ASME codes for preservice and inservice inspections, specifies that valve exercising is performed during cold shutdown conditions, and describes initial in-situ testing of the DHRS function during the first reactor module’s startup.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing continuous monitoring and control.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) comply with relevant regulatory requirements, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria, by designing, fabricating, and testing reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class 1 standards. It also clarifies the classification of connected piping and references the use of the 2013 Edition of the ASME BPVC, as endorsed by regulatory guides and rulemaking.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the use of low alloy steel, various stainless steels, and nickel-based alloys, all in accordance with relevant BPVC Section II material specifications, to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It accounts for uncertainties in flow resistance and core bypass, ensuring efficient operation, maximized electrical generation, and safety margins, while highlighting the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five on the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It specifies that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the document for regulatory purposes, and any copies must retain the copyright notice. The section also references the table of contents for Chapter Five, which covers the summary description and design basis of the system.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: Threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components use Alloy 718, chosen for its compatibility with borated water and resistance to stress corrosion cracking, in accordance with ASME BPVC standards. Preservice and inservice inspection and testing of pressure-retaining components follow ASME BPVC Section XI and relevant regulatory requirements, with inspection programs updated to the latest approved code editions for each inspection interval.

edges.description: Section A describes specific features (e.g., degasification line, RVVs) that influence RCS volume distribution, while Section B outlines the design reliance on natural circulation, connecting the volume distribution in A to the functional requirement in B.

Section A describes specific features of the RCS, such as degasification and pressure boundary integrity, which are components detailed in the broader system overview provided in Section B, thus Section A specifies details for the general description in Section B.

Section A and Section B are identical text excerpts that both provide a general description of the RCS design basis, including reliance on natural circulation and the system's major components, thus they reinforce the same foundational information for analyzing RCS volume distribution and flow path.

Section A describes the physical integration and flow path between the pressurizer and the reactor coolant system, while Section B explains how the RCS design—including the pressurizer volume—facilitates gas removal and pressure control, both supporting the analysis of how RCS volume distribution enables natural circulation.

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and flow path within the RCS, while Section B outlines the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, connecting them by showing how the pressurizer's design (A) supports the system's natural circulation function (B).

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and flow path within the RCS, while Section B provides an overview of the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, connecting the pressurizer's design (A) as a supporting detail for the system's natural circulation described in (B).

Section B describes the pressurizer's location and its flow path connection to the RCS, which is part of the overall RCS volume distribution that must be compared with the flow paths (such as those involving high-point vents discussed in Section A) to assess support for natural circulation.

Section A establishes that the RCS components are designed and constructed to meet regulatory codes for integrity, while Section B describes specific system features (like degasification and pressure retention) that operationalize and support the integrity requirements outlined in Section A.

Section A establishes that the RCS components comply with regulatory codes for integrity, while Section B describes the RCS design's reliance on natural circulation, connecting compliance with code requirements to the system's ability to safely support natural circulation as described in the analysis question.

Section A establishes that the RCS components are designed and constructed to meet regulatory codes, while Section B describes the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, with the code-compliant design from A supporting the functional description in B.

Section A specifies the physical components and materials comprising the pressurizer region of the RCS, while Section B describes the functional role of the pressurizer volume and associated flow paths in degasification and pressure boundary integrity, connecting the physical volume distribution to its operational support for natural circulation.

Section A provides detailed component descriptions (including the pressurizer and its integration with the RPV) that specify the general system boundaries and components outlined in Section B's summary of the RCS, connecting the physical volume allocations to the overall flow path necessary for natural circulation.

Section A provides detailed component descriptions (e.g., pressurizer materials and configuration) that specify the general RCS system overview and natural circulation function described in Section B, connecting the physical design to the system's operational principle.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system performance based on steady state parameters, which are determined by the RCS volumes detailed in Table 5.1-1, while Section B describes the physical configuration and flow paths of the RCS as depicted in Figure 5.1-2; together, the information from both sections connects the quantitative volume data to the qualitative flow path, supporting the analysis of natural circulation capability.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system parameters, including RCS volumes, to optimize performance and support natural circulation, while Section B describes the design basis of the RCS, emphasizing its reliance on natural circulation and the absence of pumps, thus connecting the volume distribution (from Table 5.1-1) and flow path (from Figure 5.1-2) to the system's natural circulation capability.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system parameters, including RCS volumes, to optimize performance and support natural circulation, while Section B provides a general description of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, connecting the specific volume data to the overall system function.

Section A details the material and design requirements for RCS boundary components, which ensure the integrity of the flow paths and volumes described in Section B that are essential for supporting natural circulation.

Section B describes the overall design and function of the RCS, including its reliance on natural circulation, while Section A provides specific material and design details (threaded fasteners) that support the integrity and safe operation of RCS components described in Section B.

Section A specifies the materials and design standards for RCS threaded fasteners, while Section B provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, with Section A supporting the integrity claims made in Section B.

Section B references Section A by indicating that the material descriptions for the pressurizer components are provided in Section 5.2, which details compliance with codes and code cases for the reactor coolant boundary.

Section A establishes that the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components, including those affecting RCS volume, comply with regulatory codes, while Section B discusses how system parameters, such as RCS volume distribution and flow paths, are evaluated to optimize and support natural circulation, thus Section A's compliance enables the system evaluation described in Section B.

Section A states that all RCPB components, including those with threaded fasteners described in Section B, comply with ASME BPVC requirements, establishing that the material and design details in Section B are part of the overall code compliance asserted in Section A.

Section A specifies the physical components and materials comprising the pressurizer and its integration with the RCS, while Section B evaluates how the system's design parameters, including RCS volume distribution, support optimal performance such as natural circulation, thus Section A provides detailed specifications that inform the system-level evaluation in Section B.

Section A provides detailed descriptions of specific RCS components and their materials, which are part of the broader system introduced in Section B's preface to the chapter on the Reactor Coolant System.

Section A specifies the components and materials of the pressurizer, which is part of the RCS volume distribution relevant to natural circulation, while Section B details the materials and standards for threaded fasteners used in those same RCS components, connecting the physical design to its structural integrity.

Section A provides a detailed evaluation of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) performance and volume distribution, which is further contextualized by the general scope and copyright information in Section B's preface to the chapter containing both Table 5.1-1 and Figure 5.1-2.

Section A discusses the system evaluation and performance criteria of the RCS, while Section B details the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners in the RCS boundary, with both sections connected through their focus on ensuring the integrity and performance of the RCS but without directly referencing or specifying each other's content.
